comments.body: Please explain your thought process rather than being asked for an immediate solution. For example, why sorting? That's usually a sign of performance hits

@OneCricketeer, I have updated the post with more details about my thought process, as well as why I am sorting. The issue I faced with my code is incorrect output for hidden test cases, there is no performance issue with it.
I don't think you should "try hardest first" because note the part about "only if those toys are not harder to wrap than the easiest one you've already wrapped there".

that reads like a homework/contest assignment. -- there is Theoretical Computer Science for these things. this is not programming. this is logic/theory. random engineers are bound to be fairly useless for this stuff. you need to address the theoretical CS crowd. those don't hang out here. they loathe the language lawyers and assembly microoptimizers that hang out here. tagging it "java" and presenting some code is fairly irrelevant to figuring out approaches to the problem. coding is mere implementation of the understanding.

answers.body:
An amusing wrinkle on a standard asset allocation problem.

Do not think about assigning individual toys. Think about finding and assigning the most efficient set of toys. e.g 10, 10, 9, 9 is more efficient than 10, 10, 1, 1. for a 2,2 rule.

  • Find the rule with the greatest benefit ratio free wraps / paid wraps

  • let Ns be the number of toys for that rule free wraps + paid wraps. Calculate all the possible select Ns from m ( m = the number of toys ) and find the greatest total nominal cost of free wraps / cost of paid wraps.

  • Assign and repeat.

comments.body: " tables in order of increasing requirements" Not clear what that means. Number of paid wraps? Ratio free wraps / paid wraps? Something else?
I think this answer assumes that each table can only be used once. As I understand it you could wrap everything at the same table. Although that doesn't change much.

@ravenspoint Each table has a requirement -- the minimum number of wraps you have to pay for in order to get 2 free ones.  You use the tables in order of increasing requirements, which optimally shifts the free wraps toward the heavy end of the toy list.

@maraca It assumes that, no matter what, you only get 2 free wraps at each table and it's always the 2 cheapest toys that you wrap at that table...  which seems to be implied in the question although it isn't spelled out clearly.
You need to clarify this in your answer.

answers.body:
The greedy algorithm works, if you wrap toys in order of decreasing cost and use tables in order of increasing requirements...

But when you only have one table left, you have to wrap all your remaining toys there, so at that point only the 2 cheapest remaining toys can be free.

title: Toy Wrapping with Bonus Stations
body:
This is the task description:

Imagine you have a bunch of toys that need to be wrapped as presents. Each toy takes a different amount of energy to wrap — some are easy, some are harder. You have some wrapping stations (like gift-wrapping tables), and each one has a special rule:

If you wrap a certain number of toys at that station, the station gives you 2 extra toys that you can wrap for free, but only if those toys are not harder to wrap than the easiest one you've already wrapped there.

The task is to wrap all the toys using the least amount of energy.

We can: Use any wrapping station. Wrap any toy at any station. Only use each toy once.

The Goal: Pick toys and stations in a smart way, so that you use the least total energy to wrap all the toys!

Example: You have 5 toys. Their energy needs are:

Toys = [50, 50, 30, 50, 20] You have 2 stations: Station 1: wrap 2 toys → get 2 easy ones free Station 2: wrap 3 toys → get 2 easy ones free

Smart Plan:

Use Station 1 to wrap Toy 1 and Toy 2 (each takes 50 energy). That unlocks 2 free ones! You use the free wraps on Toy 3 (30 energy) and Toy 4 (50 energy).

Wrap Toy 5 (20 energy) anywhere.

Total energy used = 50 + 50 + 20 = 120

constraints:

1 <= n <= 10^5 , n is the size of stationRules list 1 <= stationRules[i] <= 10^5 1 <= m <= 10^5, m is the size of toyWrapEffort list 1 <= toyWrapEffort[i] <= 10^4

This is what I have tried for this task:

I have used greedy approach, here is my logic:

I started by sorting the toys in descending order of energy cost. The intuition behind this was to pay for the hardest toys first — the ones with the highest energy cost — because those are the most expensive and least likely to qualify for free wrapping due to the constraint that free toys must be no harder than the easiest one paid for at the same station.

By wrapping the most expensive toys early, I hoped to unlock free wraps for cheaper toys — ideally ones that are close in effort (‘nearest hardest’), but still under the minimum threshold. This way, we reduce the total energy cost by using the free wraps on toys that would otherwise still be relatively expensive.

After running the code, I observed that while my approach passed some initial test cases, it failed 4 hidden ones out of 15. This indicated that the logic I used wasn’t fully correct or didn’t account for all edge cases. I didn’t properly enforce the condition that free toys must be no harder than the easiest paid toy at the same station. This oversight likely caused invalid toy assignments in those failing cases.

Time complexity is [O(n * log(n)) + O(m * log(m)) + m]

import java.util.*;

class Main {
    public static int wrapToysSmartly(List<Integer> stationRules, List<Integer> toyWrapEffort) {    
        Collections.sort(toyWrapEffort, Collections.reverseOrder());
        Collections.sort(stationRules);
        int m = toyWrapEffort.size(), n = stationRules.size();
        long output = 0;
        int free = 0, index = 0, count = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) {
            int e = toyWrapEffort.get(i);
            if (free > 0) {
                free--;
            } else {
                output += e;
                count++;
                
                // if we collected enough toys the move to next station and collect next 2 free toys
                if (index < n && count >= stationRules.get(index)) {
                    free += 2;
                    count -= stationRules.get(index);
                    index++;
                }
            }
        }
        return (int) output;
    }

    public static void main(String... args) {
        System.out.println(wrapToysSmartly(Arrays.asList(2, 3), Arrays.asList(50, 50, 30, 50, 20))); // Output: 120
        System.out.println(wrapToysSmartly(Arrays.asList(5, 5, 2), Arrays.asList(48, 75, 20, 46, 33))); // Output: 143
        System.out.println(wrapToysSmartly(Arrays.asList(4, 4, 7), Arrays.asList(64, 38, 95, 34, 33, 96, 10, 59))); // Output: 357
    }
}

I added 3 test cases in the main method to check my code, and it worked for those. But when I ran it on Hackerrank, 4 out of 15 test cases failed. The ones that failed are hidden, so I can’t see what went wrong.

What is the correct approach to solve this task in less time complexity.
